title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the map. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Mapping the World: The Evolution of Cartography
content.text: Maps are much more than just navigation tools; they are a reflection of how we perceive our place in the universe. In ancient times, a map was as much a work of art and theology as it was a geographical record. The earliest known world maps, such as the Babylonian *Imago Mundi*, showed the world as a flat disc surrounded by a 'bitter river' of salt water. ||(41)|| [41]This transition from myth to measurement began with the Greeks[/41]. Claudius Ptolemy, working in Alexandria during the 2nd century AD, introduced the concept of a grid system based on latitude and longitude. His *Geographia* was the first to use mathematical principles to project the curved surface of the Earth onto a flat piece of parchment. ||(42)|| [42]However, much of this classical knowledge was lost to Europe during the Middle Ages[/42]. Maps became symbolic again, with 'T-O maps' placing Jerusalem at the very center of the world and the garden of Eden at the top. It wasn't until the Renaissance that the focus shifted back to practical exploration. ||(43)|| [43]This sudden expansion of the known world presented a major problem for cartographers: how to show the vast, newly discovered continents without distorting their shape or size[/43]. The solution came in 1569 from Gerardus Mercator, who developed a projection that allowed sailors to plot a straight-line course using a compass. ||(44)|| [44]While the Mercator projection was a godsend for navigation, it had a significant flaw: it drastically exaggerated the size of landmasses near the poles[/44]. This meant that Europe and North America appeared much larger than Africa or South America, a distortion that has influenced our political perception of the world for centuries. ||(45)|| [45]The 20th century brought another radical shift with the advent of aerial photography and satellite imagery[/45]. We no longer had to rely on the reports of explorers on the ground; we could see the entire planet from above. This led to the creation of 'orthophoto' maps, which combined the detail of a photograph with the accuracy of a traditional chart. ||(46)|| [46]Today, the map has moved from paper to the palm of our hand[/46]. Digital mapping and GPS have made it almost impossible to get lost, yet they have also changed our relationship with the landscape. We often follow the blue dot on a screen without ever looking up to see the world around us. Cartography is no longer about discovering the unknown, but about managing the data of the known.

paragraphs.F: Indeed, [41] these early depictions [/41] were meant to explain the cosmos rather than guide a traveler. They placed the heavens and the underworld on the same page as the local mountains and rivers, creating a unified picture of reality.
paragraphs.D: This [42] scientific approach [/42] was revolutionary, but it was limited by the data available at the time. Ptolemy believed the Indian Ocean was a landlocked sea and that Africa was much smaller than it actually is, errors that would persist for over a millennium.
paragraphs.A: The [43] arrival of the Age of Discovery [/43] in the 15th century forced a total rethink of the world map. As Portuguese and Spanish ships returned with news of the Americas and a direct route to the Indies, the old religious charts became obsolete overnight.
paragraphs.E: This [44] breakthrough in geometry [/44] made the map an essential tool for the expanding colonial empires. It allowed for the safe crossing of the Atlantic and Pacific, turning the ocean from a barrier into a highway for trade and conquest.
paragraphs.B: This [45] geographical bias [/45] has been the subject of much debate in modern times. Critics argue that by making the northern hemisphere look dominant, these maps reinforced the colonial mindset and marginalized the cultures of the 'Global South'.
paragraphs.C: This [46] technological leap [/46] culminated in the development of GIS (Geographic Information Systems). These digital tools allow us to layer vast amounts of data—from traffic patterns to climate change—onto a single map, making them vital for urban planning and environmental protection.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] modern cartographers [/47] still appreciate the aesthetic beauty of hand-drawn maps. They argue that while digital maps are more accurate, they lack the 'soul' and the narrative quality that made ancient charts so fascinating to study.

questions.explanation: F refers back to the 'early depictions' like the Babylonian world map and explains their theological purpose.
D links the 'scientific approach' to Ptolemy's grid system and latitude/longitude, mentioned before the gap.
A explains how the 'Age of Discovery' and the return of ships from the Americas made the old maps obsolete.
E identifies Mercator's projection as a 'breakthrough in geometry' that facilitated ocean crossing and colonial trade.
B expands on the 'geographical bias' of the Mercator projection and its political impact, as described before the gap.
C describes the 'technological leap' involving GIS, following the mention of satellite imagery and aerial photography.
